data.title: No Guts, No Glory - 2 Of My Favorite Dividend Stocks For Serious Wealth
data.description:
Introduction

I have mentioned it in a few articles, but I am absolutely fascinated by Las Vegas, a city I have actually never visited, as weird as that may sound.

There's something about the mix of ultra-luxury hotels, world-class restaurants, artists trying to start a career, people fleeing Southern California's expensive real estate market, and 'degenerate' gamblers trying their luck.

Some of these gamblers have found a way to make a lot of money without creating a winning track record, which is nearly impossible anyway.

Vegas Matt has become one of the city's most notorious gamblers, as he started a social media account based on letting people watch him place bets of up to tens of thousands of dollars on table games like blackjack.

Initially, he lost money placing big bets. However, it paid off, as the number of people watching him on social media exploded. Eventually, these views now pay for his expensive lifestyle, on top of partnerships and the many benefits he gets from casinos. After all, casinos LOVE the promotion.

The reason I'm bringing this up is because he isn't doing this to suck people into a gambling lifestyle. It's just entertainment, and he tracks all of his bets.

Here's a screenshot from one of his most recent videos, which shows that he has lost nearly $50,000 on a year-to-date basis.

Vegas Matt knew he could build a business out of it, while most of his followers have the right mindset, as they know it's a money-losing hobby. The odds are ALWAYS against us in a casino, which is how these operators make money.

It's a win-win-win, except the 'win' for most gamblers is entertainment.

This brings me to the stock market, as having the right mindset is absolutely critical, which caused me to write this article. You need to know exactly what you are getting into, as obvious as that may sound.

See, on a short-term basis, investing in the stock market is somewhat similar to gambling, with the major difference that gambling is all-or-nothing.

  • Historically speaking, the probability of generating a profit when buying the S&P 500 for a single day is 51%, which is basically like flipping a coin.
  • On a one-week basis, that probability rises to 57%.
  • On a one-year basis, that number is 74%.
  • No investor has ever lost money investing in equities on a 20-year rolling basis since 1950.

It's not hard to understand why I, being a long-term investor, like these odds.

This doesn't apply to gambling. In fact, the more time you spend in a casino, the more likely you are to lose money, as the odds of every single spin (or whatever game you're playing) are against you.

However, it's not free money, which is why I brought up mindset.

Long-term investing isn't for the faint of heart or anyone without strong conviction. Using more statistics, since 1950, the chances of experiencing a bear market of at least 20% stock market weakness every two years are close to 50%.

Every year, the change of a bear market is 32%.

On a ten-year basis, the odds are almost 100%.

In other words, the longer we stay in the market, the higher the probability we make money and the higher the probability we encounter bear markets that (temporarily) reduce our capital by at least 20%.

The flip side is becoming active investors, which allows us to avoid bear markets but requires us to be world-class market timers. Historically speaking, active trading is a fast way to lose money, as I wrote in a detailed article.

There's also a third way, which is defensive investing. This brings me to the core of this article.

Defensive Investing Comes At A Price

Whenever markets enter turmoil, 'everyone' starts to talk about the need to invest in defensive investments. In a recent article (I cannot remember which one), I brought this up as well, comparing defensive investing to learning how to swim. Usually (ideally), we learn how to swim when we're young, well ahead of any situation where we're required to swim.

Defensive investing is similar, as I believe it is a horrible idea to shift one's focus to defensive investments DURING a period of turmoil. While I am by no means saying that these stocks are the worst investments imaginable that need to be ignored (I love PepsiCo (PEP) at these levels), I am making the case that we don't all suddenly need to become defensive the moment the S&P 500 enters some rough waters.

As it turns out, my thesis is getting some support, as The Wall Street Journal just published a piece titled "Funds Promising Shelter From Wild Swings Are Booming. But Do They Deliver?"

For starters, defensive investing has become very popular, which I understand, as many (older) investors who have enjoyed many decades of elevated capital gains are more likely to protect their assets at this point in their lives.

Hence, it makes sense to see a massive uptrend in the assets managed by equity-hedged ETFs. According to Morningstar, these funds now hold $56 billion in AUM, with more than 24 new funds entering the market since 2024.

These funds apply a wide range of strategies that make them defensive.

Many use option strategies, including put options, which give them the right to sell a stock at a pre-determined price. If the actual stock price is lower, they have successfully limited losses.

The problem is that these strategies are expensive, as buying options comes with a premium. Also, all of these activities require active management, which means higher salaries to attract talented investment managers.

As a result, many of these defensive ETFs and mutual funds cost more than 1.00% per year, which is a mile above the low-single-digit costs of index funds like the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), which costs a mere 0.03% per year.

The worst part, however, is the poor performance of these funds. As we can see below, downside protection of funds like the Invesco S&P 500 Downside Hedged ETF was limited this year. It also missed the market's rebound, lagging the S&P 500 by more than 750 basis points on a year-to-date basis.

The same applies to the JPMorgan Hedged Equity Fund (JHEQX), which is the largest defensive fund, with more than $20 billion in assets. This fund invests in large-cap stocks in the U.S., while using options to limit downside risk.

It has a five-star rating from Morningstar, which, in addition to its massive AUM, proves that it is a very popular place for investors to put their money.

However, it's not that great.

Over the past five years, it has returned 10.3% per year. While that is a high number, it has lagged the S&P 500 by more than 800 basis points per year.

Hence, despite its much lower volatility, it has a risk-adjusted return that's slightly lower than the S&P 500.

Moreover, using the long-term ratio between JHEQX and the S&P 500 (see below), we can conclude that the fund has consistently underperformed the market, with some peaks during periods of turmoil (i.e., in 2020).

While I am no financial advisor, which means I cannot speak for anyone reading this, I see absolutely no reason to invest in these ETFs.

I also see no reason to go massively overweight in defensive stocks during a sell-off, as that's when we should be focusing on less defensive stocks, which often offer many attractive opportunities for long-term investors when everyone is looking the other way.

Having said all of this, I'm no idiot. This article isn't an attempt to get people to ignore safe investments. I know that many of my readers are retired, which means they have totally different risk profiles compared to younger investors.

Hence, in light of everything we have discussed, I decided to focus on two dividend stocks that bring something special to the table.

  • Both are cyclical yet far from risky, which is one important requirement of any stock I buy during times of turmoil.
  • Both companies have terrific business models that make them stand out.
  • I expect both stocks to consistently grow their dividends in the future.
  • All things considered, I expect both companies to beat the market on a long-term basis.

So, as this theoretical framework already took me 1,500 words to write, let's get to the first pick, which has a low yield but a total return profile that makes up for it.

Pool Corporation (POOL) - When Housing Is Down, I'm Getting Interested

Right now, the housing market is a hot mess.

Because of elevated mortgage rates and the Fed's failure to cool home prices, existing home sales have fallen off a cliff in recent years. Currently, existing home sales are 40-50% below normal pre-pandemic levels, as most people are simply priced out of the market.

This is bad news for a wide range of companies, including DIY/home improvement retailers like Home Depot (HD) and Lowe's (LOW), realtors, construction suppliers, lenders, and specialized distributors.

One of these companies is the Pool Corporation, which is the world's largest wholesale distributor of swimming pool and related backyard products.

The company serves professional contractors, service providers, and do-it-yourself pool owners through its massive network of almost 450 wholesale distribution centers and roughly 300 Pinch A Penny franchise stores.

Roughly 70% of its markets are serviced through distribution channels.

While the company does not build homes, the pool industry is highly related to housing, as new home sales often accelerate investments in pool renovations and similar operations. On top of that, the majority of its sales come from maintenance operations, which is highly beneficial, as pools require upkeep.

This makes it less susceptible to housing downturns.

Its portfolio includes maintenance chemicals, pool equipment, building materials, and everything else you can imagine that is required to build and maintain pools, ranging from cheap entry models to the most luxurious ones in places like Beverly Hills, California.

It also needs to be said that it's still a booming business, with new pool construction being a $2.5 billion market. Renovation is a $3.0 billion market. These markets grew by 15% and 19% per year since 2019, respectively, with the biggest growth driver being the favorable post-pandemic years, when the housing market boomed and people moved out of the cities into the suburbs.

Additionally, the company is moving into commercial pools, which account for just 4% of its current revenue. This market could grow by 10-15% per year.

On a long-term basis, Pool Corp aims for 6% to 9% annual sales growth, which is expected to be driven by industry-related factors like pool additions and new home sales, as well as differentiators like streamlined distribution, better online channels, private label brands, and more.

It's quite impressive that M&A is expected to account for less than 1% of annual growth, with new products and market share gains being the biggest driver of up to 3% above-market growth.

Even since 2019, inflation has not been the biggest growth driver, as 73% of revenue growth since 2019 was provided by M&A, new products, and a stronger installed base, which beat the $1.0 billion pricing gain.

With regard to its dividend, Pool Corporation shares yield 1.7%. This yield comes with a 45% payout ratio and a five-year CAGR of 16.8%, a number that beats the consumer discretionary median of 7.8% by a wide margin.

On top of that, it is an aggressive share repurchaser, as roughly half of its free cash flow went toward buybacks this year (so far). Both dividends and buybacks dwarf CapEx, as the company is increasingly leveraging its existing assets, which is highly beneficial for long-term dividend growth.

Valuation-wise, we're seeing good news.

The company currently trades at 27.2x earnings. While that may seem like an elevated number, it's below the 10-year average of 31.1x and supported by elevated long-term growth and favorable tailwinds.

In 2021, the stock traded at more than 40x earnings.

Since then, the stock has come down, pressured by declining earnings.

While 2025 is expected to see a 1% EPS contraction, it's expected to be the bottom, with high-single-digit expected EPS growth in both 2026 and 2027.

While POOL remains cyclical, it's the kind of dividend grower that makes a LOT of sense to buy during market weakness, as it has a highly underappreciated business model and the ability to deliver outsized returns for many years to come.

The only reason why it is not currently on my watchlist is because of my housing exposure through stocks like Carlisle Companies (CSL) and Home Depot - among others with lower housing-related revenues.

And to get to the main point of this article, because of its smart business model, Pool Corp is the kind of business one can own 'forever', using market turmoil to expand positions.

The same goes for the next stock.

Union Pacific Corp. (UNP) - I Buy More On Every Correction

I wasn't sure about including Union Pacific in this article, as I have brought it up quite frequently in recent years.

However, to me, Union Pacific is the embodiment of the perfect, reliable cyclical stock that makes for perfect buying opportunities during economic turmoil.

It absolutely needs to be part of this article.

My most recent article that included Union Pacific was written on May 19, titled "Buried Under Fear: One Of The Best Opportunities I've Seen In Years."

The article was about a similar topic, as I discussed cyclical weakness and my expectations that this offers tremendous opportunities for long-term investors.

My point is that I expect this unloved sector to deliver substantial alpha, which provides opportunities in a lot of equities, from trusted dividend growers like railroads and machinery stocks to smaller cyclical gems that have the potential to double or even triple.

The same applies to energy stocks as well, but that's something for a different article.

For now, I will be watching cyclical stocks extremely closely because if we are indeed in a position similar to Trump's first term in 2016/2017, we could be looking at substantial alpha and some terrific entries for long-term positions in reliable dividend growers, even if we continue to deal with headwinds in the short to mid-term.

Union Pacific is perfect because it isn't just a random stock with a cyclical growth profile. It is what I like to call an ultra-wide moat company, enjoying a duopoly in the Western two-thirds of the United States.

Together with Buffett-owned BNSF, it dominates bulk, merchandise, and intermodal transportation, serving all major ports, economic hubs, and connections to Canada, Mexico, and the U.S. East Coast.

Especially the decision to make Jim Vena CEO a few years ago has turned out to be a great decision, as Union Pacific is now the most efficient Class I railroad in North America, lowering its operating ratio to 60.0%, a number that is especially impressive in light of sticky inflation and poor demand.

Now, the company sees a path to a 58% operating ratio, driven by factors like efficient capital allocation, a headcount reduction of roughly 7% supported by higher efficiencies, and better service levels, as the company currently has an intermodal service performance index of 94, up from 76 in 2022.

Despite reducing the employee count, investments in technology and better tracking have allowed the company to improve operating metrics across the board, which helps it to grow the business, benefit from economic re-shoring, and improve pricing power.

In fact, 1Q25 pricing has been the best in more than a decade, as the company explained during the Bank of America Industrials, Transportation, and Airlines Conference last month.

These developments not only sound good but are supported by more data, as the company has an industry-leading ROIC, meaning it is highly successful when it comes to investing in its own business, finding high-return opportunities that generate shareholder value.

As it has a weighted average cost of capital of 4.2%, it is essentially generating a spread of more than 10%, which has allowed it to consistently boost shareholder value.

Currently yielding 2.5% with a 47% payout ratio, Union Pacific has hiked its dividend for 18 consecutive years. During this period, it has bought back 44% of its shares, which substantially improved the per-share value of the business.

As a result, the company has consistently beaten the market between the early 2000s and 2021. Due to cyclical headwinds, since then, the ratio between UNP and the S&P 500 has been in a downtrend.

That's why I consider UNP to be such a great buying opportunity on weakness, making it one of my favorite investments during times of trouble.

Takeaway

I'm a strong believer in the long-term odds of the stock market, where the probability of positive returns rises to 74% over a year and to 100% over 20 years.

Yet, bear markets are inevitable, with a 32% chance of encountering one every single year. This basically guarantees a bear market every decade.

While that may be annoying, it's also where opportunities come from.

As I explained in this article, I don't chase defensive stocks during turmoil, as that decision can be costly, especially in light of the better opportunities that high-quality cyclical stocks tend to provide during weakness.

In this article, I presented two of them.

Pool Corporation thrives in the pool industry with a 1.7% yield and 16.8% long-term annual dividend growth, supported by a strong distribution network, which is part of a genius business model.

Meanwhile, Union Pacific, which I consider an ultra-wide-moat railroad, offers a 2.5% yield and unmatched efficiency, putting it in a great spot to benefit from an economic rebound.

To me, these are my kind of long-term winners, best bought on dips for potentially market-beating long-term returns.

Risks To My Thesis

My thesis depends on cyclical stocks like POOL and UNP outperforming during market rebounds. Hence, prolonged economic downturns, persistent inflation, or unexpected disruptions in housing or rail demand could delay their recovery and hurt my thesis.

That's the risk of buying cyclical stocks and the importance of focusing on quality.

data.title: GPIQ: Promising Blueprint But Hold For Now
data.description:
I have, in my earlier analyses, covered ETFs based on option writing for income generation, over a broad portfolio trying to emulate the NDX. Of those, QQQI looked better than JEPQ in terms of performance in correcting markets. While in theory there was little to choose from between the two's investment and option strategy, the active management of the option strategy may have led to performance differences, which could even change in the future.

However, the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:GPIQ) seems to have differences in principle that should lead to different results. First of all, it aims to replicate the NDX as much as possible, so expectations of deviations from the NDX are low. That could be good or bad depending on how effective the deviations are for other ETFs not following NDX as closely, but GPIQ's holdings look more systematic and take out active portfolio composition.

The second difference in principle is the dynamic call writing leverage. The notional value of options written varies from 25%-75% of the holdings' total exposure. This is a clear deviation from most other income ETFs, who typically don't mention a dynamic capping of upside potential. The dynamic nature of notional option writing exposure, along with the fact that a maximum of 75% of the portfolio's upside is capped, makes the offering a standout one.

The Difference in GPIQ

To understand why GPIQ's structure is a standout, I will quickly recap my general stand on option writing ETFs. I have highlighted in my previous writings, how the capped upside usually leads to lower total return in the long term for option writing ETFs. Meaning, investors riding the underlying directly and generating the same income as the income ETF will end up with more capital appreciation. In that case, the only significant gain in using an income vehicle is if it offers lower volatility and drawdowns to compensate for overall lower returns. However, structurally, unless the options are rolled down actively during a correction, the downside mitigation is not commensurate to the upside potential lost. Most income ETFs are rigid around waiting on an at-the-money or near-the-money call option for the entire tenure till expiry. This provides limited downside protection. As crashes in the recent past have been limited, such impacts have not been visible, particularly on broad indices. But the structural risk of capping a 15% bull run in the underlying over the month to 2% exists. The same income fund is likely to show a net drawdown of 30% if the index goes down 35%.

Given the structural issues, a fund that does not really cap the upside fully, but only reduces it, has a chance to perform better because some part of the capital (25%-75% in the case of GPIQ) is always exposed to the full extent of the bull run. Whether that translates to real performance benefits or not is an empirical study, depending on how well GPIQ actively judges how much portfolio to cap. Such limited upside capping can be replicated through call spreads as well, and some ETFs use that, but a minimum of 25% notional exposure opens up capital appreciation opportunities a lot more.

The Empirical Performance Study

First, we will look at how total returns have fared against the index as well as peers in the common period of existence. My expectations of GPIQ being able to match QQQ (proxying NDX) better than alternative option strategies are not met overall, though the delta is small in comparison with QQQI. Below is how a $10k investment would have grown for GPIQ and peers, benchmarks.

For practical purposes, the above tenure is across bull rallies, corrections, and bounce-backs, so overall performance here is a good enough indicator of what to expect. However, let's double-click on a bull rally and a correction phase separately to understand better. In the below rebound phase, the upside cap has not disturbed the total returns of QQQI and GPIQ by much, but differences magnify over time. There is hardly anything to choose between them as it stands, though. GPIQ does do better than QQQI, but only by a small margin. Limited data (i.e., tenure under observation) doesn't make the findings conclusive, but significant performance differences should still have come up if they were structurally available.

Downside protection is also not conclusive because of the short tenure of analysis, but GPIQ shows more drawdown than either of JEPQ or QQQI.

The impact of the length of analysis, which I could attempt for my separate analysis for JPEQ, is very critical. JEPQ over a longer time period had actually shown clearly that its pitfall is the falling market and downside protection. Here we see the opposite effect. It still shows structural issues and hence underperformance overall. QQQI, on the other hand, shows how closely it is able to track NDX. Core findings of my prior analyses.

With the limited data, GPIQ becomes a difficult call. I still like the dynamic capping of upsides in theory and believe in its ability to prevent NAV erosion and give better total returns (while sustaining income). However, the performance so far does not beat an alternative approach from QQQI.

Investment Advice

A dividend yield of ~10-12% historically is stable and reasonable. A good balance between payouts and de-risking from the underlying vs keeping enough on the table for capital appreciation. The NAV erosion is not as extreme as aggressive income payouts usually result in (like some YieldMax products designed to de-risk faster). However, conceptually the case is the same for QQQI and JEPQ as well.

Before moving on to my conclusion, it is important to understand that most of these income vehicles are very similar in performance. The differences are minor and change with time, given its active nature. However, JPIQ underwhelms its promise of a better theoretical structure, looking at performance so far. My benchmark of playing NDX for income is QQQI. JEPQ certainly does not breach that standard. GPIQ appears to be reasonable in bull markets, in comparison. However, I see drawdowns worse than JEPQ in bear markets. I wish I had more data to check this conclusively. The drawdown mitigation is extremely critical for an income plan, and GPIQ does not certainly show great performance there to earn a Buy (certainly not commensurate to any outperformance in bull markets). However, I will keep GPIQ on a hold, given its short tenure (JEPQ had a larger conclusive dataset to conclude a sell). I am also keeping some theoretical hope for GPIQ on the basis of its structural promise.

data.title: Argan: I Was Horribly Wrong (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Even the most seasoned investors make mistakes. I'm talking about the Warren Buffetts of the world. If they make mistakes, you can imagine that experienced investors who are less seasoned, such as myself with 17 years of experience under my belt, will certainly make mistakes as well. One of mine involves a company by the name of Argan (NYSE:AGX), which serves as a construction company that caters to the power industry, as well as other aspects of the industrial economy. From August 2023 until December of last year, I had the company rated a 'buy'. And during that time, shares of the business skyrocketed 289.3%. That was massively above the 35.2% rise that the S&P 500 saw over that same window of time. But because of the valuation of the company, I decided to downgrade it late last year.

That was, unfortunately, a big mistake. Powered by significant growth in revenue, profits, cash flows, and backlog, the company has seen its share price move up another 46.9% at a time when the S&P 500 is down 1.9%. Shares do look rather expensive if we use historical results from last year. But if we annualize the performance that we have seen recently, they actually look pretty decent. The recent data that I'm referring to involves the first quarter of the firm's 2026 fiscal year, which just came out after the market closed on June 4th. Management demonstrated significant growth across the board. That on top of this the strong balance sheet that the company boasts, and I believe that upgrading it back to a soft 'buy' is necessary.

A Great Quarter

As I mentioned, after the market closed on June 4th, the management team at Argan announced financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company totaled $193.7 million. That represented an increase of 22.8% compared to the $157.7 million generated one year earlier. It also happened to be in line with what analysts were anticipating for the quarter.

This growth for the company was driven by a 45.4% surge in revenue from its Power Industry Services segment, with revenue shooting up from $110.3 million to $160.4 million. In case you are new to the company, this is the part of the business that serves as a full service EPC services firm that is engaged in the designing, building, and commissioning of large-scale energy projects, with examples being combined cycle facilities, liquefied natural gas power island projects, biomass plants, solar fields, battery storage projects, wind farms, and more. During the quarter, the company benefited from increased activities associated with the 405 MW Midwest Solar Project, the Louisiana LNG Facility, and the Trumbull Energy Center that it has been working on. Things would have been better had it not been for decreased construction activities involving other projects such as its Midwest Solar and Battery Projects, the Shannonbridge Power Project, and the ESB FlexGen Peaker Plants since those have been either partially or completely completed.

The company also saw a 10.8% rise involving its Telecommunications Infrastructure Services segment, but those sales still accounted for only $4.1 million, up from $3.7 million last year. That makes those operations barely worth mentioning. This is not to say that everything went well. In fact, year over year, the firm did see a 33.2% plunge in revenue associated with its Industrial Construction Services segment. That pushed revenue down from $43.7 million to $29.2 million. According to management, this decline was because of a drop in the amount of field services construction activities and the vessel fabrication work on a year-over-year basis.

In addition to doing well from a revenue growth perspective, the company also did very well when it came to profitability. Year over year, earnings per share skyrocketed from $0.58 to $1.60, taking net profits up from only $7.9 million to $22.6 million. The earnings per share reported by the company happened to be a whopping $0.51 greater than what analysts were anticipating. Some of this improvement came from the fact that revenue increased. However, the company also benefited from the fact that selling, general, and administrative costs dropped from 7.25% of sales to 6.47%. This might not seem like much of an improvement. But when applied to the revenue generated during the quarter, that's an extra $1.5 million in pre-tax profits for the business. Even better than this was the fact that its gross profit margin shot up from 11.38% to 19.03%. Some of this was because of the absence of a hit associated with losses on an overseas project that the company endured last year that hit profitability to the tune of $2.6 million. But most of the change came from a favorable mix of projects and contract types compared to what the company saw in its 2025 fiscal year.

Other profitability metrics rose as well. Take operating cash flow as an example. From the first quarter of 2025 to the first quarter of 2026, it more than doubled from $17.7 million to $35.9 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $14.1 million to $25.9 million. Lastly, EBITDA for the business expanded from $11.9 million to $30.3 million. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2024 and 2025 fiscal years in their entirety. This shows that the recent growth that the company has achieved has not been a blip on the radar. Instead, it has been part of a larger trend for the company as it benefits from energy projects.

The good news for shareholders is that the growth here is likely just beginning. For starters, the backlog for the company continues to expand nicely. In the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, the backlog was $1.86 billion. That was far greater than the $824 million reported the previous year. It also represented a nice move higher compared to the $1.36 billion in backlog that the company ended the 2025 fiscal year with.

Management has talked about certain key industries as major growth areas for the business. Perhaps the most talked about nowadays would be the AI and data center business. Generative AI investment has skyrocketed in recent years. According to one source, it is estimated that global investments in data centers aimed at scaling AI was around $455 billion last year. That's 51% greater than what it was in 2022. And if everything goes according to plan, it's expected for spending to grow another 30% this year. By the year 2029, it's anticipated that capital expenditures in this space will exceed $1 trillion annually. Honestly, I don't see how this growth does not come to fruition. Take technology behemoth Apple (AAPL) as an example. Not long ago, it announced that it planned to spend $500 billion over the next four years on things like AI infrastructure, data centers, and research and development in silicon engineering. For its part, management has said that the average annual growth rate involving AI power should be around 70% through the year 2027.

There is also the prospect of additional growth involving electric vehicles and charging stations. By the year 2040, it's anticipated that electric vehicle adoption will grow twentyfold, raising the average total annual electric vehicle load in the US to 468,000 GWh compared to the 24,000 GWh in 2023. Solar projects and battery projects should also see growth thanks to both private industry and government spending. This has been a long time in the making, with companies committing $525 billion towards the construction of factories that make semiconductors, microchips, batteries, and solar panels, since the year 2021. There is also the prospect of retirements associated with old energy infrastructure. This year, for instance, 12.3 GW of power is expected to be retired. That's up from the 7.4 GW that ended up being retired last year. And over the next 10 years, 78 GW of planned retirements have already been announced.

Unfortunately, we don't know what to expect when it comes to the rest of this year from a profit perspective. Management has not really provided any detailed figures here. But if we annualize the results that we have seen so far for the year, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of $183.1 million and EBITDA of $289 million. Using these estimates, and using the historical performance from 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. On a historical basis, shares are very expensive. But when we use the forward estimates, the stock looks reasonably priced. In the table below, I then compared it to five similar firms. And what I found was that, relative to these other players, shares are not priced at levels that are unreasonable. Using the 2025 figures, they are perhaps a bit on the pricey side of the spectrum. But using the 2024 estimates, the picture is mixed, with the firm actually being cheaper than four of the five companies on an EV to EBITDA basis.

Company Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Argan - 2025 29.9 21.4
Argan - 2026 est. 16.3 8.4
MYR Group (MYRG) 16.1 20.2
Tutor Perini Corporation (TPC) 4.8 54.4
Ameresco (AMRC) 11.5 12.1
Limbach Holdings (LMB) 37.1 27.6
Great Lakes Dredge & Dock Corporation (GLDD) 8.5 7.8

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I must say that I made a mistake when I downgraded Argan late last year. The company is doing incredibly well and there is no reason why its growth should slow down anytime soon. Add on top of this the fact that the business has no debt and enjoys a net cash position of $546.5 million, which is a monumental amount of cash for a firm with a market capitalization of only $2.98 billion, and I believe that upgrading it to a soft 'buy' makes sense.

data.title: Mid-Year Perspectives 2025: Cutting Through The Noise
data.description:
By Seema Shah

After one of the most volatile stretches in recent memory, the middle of 2025 offers an important moment to pause and take stock. Over the past few months, forecasts have been made and revised, policy announcements have been introduced and then walked back, and markets have swung between fear and optimism. Whiplash isn’t just a metaphor—it’s the lived experience of investors trying to make sense of it all.

Amid the noise, a clearer picture is starting to emerge. The U.S. economy remains resilient but is slowing, policy uncertainty continues to cast a long shadow, and markets are adjusting to a more complicated set of signals—rising fiscal stress, sticky inflation, and shifting global dynamics. With tariff and tax decisions still pending, visibility remains low beyond the next few months, but the near-term contours of the macro and market path are beginning to take shape.

1. A resilient economy, but growth headwinds are building

Trade tensions have undoubtedly taken a toll. Escalating tariff uncertainty has delayed corporate investment, frozen hiring plans, and weighed on both business and consumer sentiment. And yet, despite this, the U.S. economy has so far avoided recession, supported by the continued resilience of household and corporate balance sheets as well as the lingering momentum from a strong first quarter.

Our base case remains for the U.S. to narrowly avoid recession as the economy navigates persistent policy uncertainty. Encouragingly, this forecast was intact even before the U.S.-China trade truce was announced in early May, as we assumed that the U.S. administration would recognize the risks and back away from the economic ledge.

2. Tariff uncertainty persists—and may worsen

Tariff noise is likely to persist over the coming months. Although the Court of International Trade unanimously ruled against President Trump’s tariffs, initially imposed through the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), it is unlikely to be the end of the tariff story. The administration has indicated that it will appeal the decision, and if that fails, it has other avenues it can pursue to ensure that Trump’s tariff agenda takes effect. If anything, tariff uncertainty will likely continue.

Should the administration succeed in reasserting tariffs, the average rate could settle at 12.5%—substantially down from the 28% level before the announced U.S.-China trade truce, but still well above the 2% level that prevailed at the start of the year. Note, too, that the average tariff rate is still biased higher. Not only has Trump not announced additional sectoral tariffs, but his recent threat to raise European Union tariffs to 50% emphasizes the risk that tariffs may settle at a higher level once the 90-day reprieve ends.

3. The Fed walks a tightrope

The Federal Reserve is navigating a narrow path. While they expect the economy to soften, persistent trade uncertainty is ripe ground for monetary policy missteps, particularly when inflation is already running above target and expected to see a tariff-induced boost in Q3, and economic data remains resilient. Many analysts have argued that the Fed should focus on the full employment side of its mandate and re-start rate cuts immediately. Yet, with both large and small businesses indicating that they plan to hold on to their workers and ride out the tariff storm, only a modest weakening in the jobs market is likely, further reducing the urgency for Fed support. We expect the Fed to wait until Q4 before it reduces policy rates.

4. Growth-oriented policy: Deregulation and fiscal expansion

Two growth-friendly policy measures may help mitigate trade-related headwinds. First, a renewed push for deregulation—particularly in the energy and financial sectors—echoes pro-growth efforts from Trump’s first term and could provide an important boost to economic growth. Second, modest fiscal expansion is on the table. While its impact may be somewhat muted, combined, these growth-friendly measures should help to offset the negative headwinds from tariffs.

5. Fiscal stimulus: Modest in impact, risky in optics

The passing of the administration’s major tax and spending legislation, which largely involves extending the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, is predominantly a continuation of existing policy and would not provide a new boost to economic growth. Alongside some incremental modest tax cuts, which would be funded by tariff revenues and some spending pullback, not only is the direct economic boost expected to be fairly modest, but, more importantly, the fiscal package is unlikely to meaningfully improve the U.S.’s long-term fiscal trajectory. Moody's recent downgrade of U.S. debt underscores that fiscal credibility remains under pressure and that market concerns regarding the U.S. fiscal picture are likely to persist.

6. Bond market tensions: Long yields up, short yields anchored

In recent weeks, renewed fiscal fears, alongside improved U.S. growth forecasts and sticky inflation concerns, put upward pressure on long-term U.S. Treasury bond yields. With bond vigilantes circling, markets are likely to demand spending cuts that are large enough or economic growth that is strong enough to bring the deficit under control, suggesting that long-term Treasury yields are likely to remain elevated over the near term. By contrast, the slowing economy and the prospect of Fed rate cuts later in 2025 should maintain downward pressure on short-term Treasury yields.

7. Equities recover—But risks remain

Equity markets have almost entirely recoupled their losses from earlier in the year, with the S&P 500 posting its strongest monthly gain in 18 months in May. Yet, the recovery seems at odds with an economic backdrop characterized by slower growth, higher inflation, and lingering policy uncertainty. Indeed, with the effects of earlier tariff uncertainty likely to begin manifesting in economic and inflation data over the coming month, near-term volatility is expected to persist as these tensions play out.

8. Higher yields pressure equity valuations

In addition to deteriorating economic data, another key vulnerability for the equity market may be the bond market itself. Although equity returns and bond yields are typically positively correlated, if the rise in yields is due to fiscal concerns, the correlation can turn negative, weighing on equity returns as the potential for multiple expansion becomes increasingly strained. As a result, persistent debt and deficit fears that drive up bond yields may test the equity market.

9. A Dollar under pressure

One of the most notable shifts in markets in recent months has been the decline in the U.S. dollar, which has defied both interest rate differentials and the traditional rush for safe havens during times of heightened volatility. With weakness reflecting concerns over the domestic outlook and institutional credibility, a further weakening is possible. Not only is the USD still overvalued, but heightened tariff policy uncertainty, lingering fiscal woes, and growing animosity toward the U.S. are likely persistent headwinds.

10. U.S. dominance: Structurally intact

While there is cyclical risk associated with the U.S., the structural investment case—which has driven outperformance in recent years—remains compelling. The long-term drivers—technology leadership, innovation, deregulation, and productivity—are still very much in play. Moreover, in the absence of a credible reserve currency alternative, U.S. exposure will remain a foundational component of global portfolios.

Implications for investors

The first half of 2025 has tested investor conviction, challenged assumptions, and reinforced just how quickly the macro and market narrative can shift. While the outlook beyond the next few months remains clouded by unresolved policy decisions, the core dynamics—slower growth, persistent inflation, elevated fiscal risk—are now more visible.

Heading into the back half of the year, portfolios should tilt toward resilience and selectivity. As such, investors should consider:

  • Short-duration, high-quality fixed income for stability and income.
  • Equities with pricing power, strong margins, and clean balance sheets, especially in sectors benefiting from deregulation or insulated from trade frictions.
  • Global diversification to hedge dollar weakness and access non-U.S. growth opportunities.
  • Active management to identify mispriced risk and sector rotation opportunities in an increasingly idiosyncratic environment.

Even in periods of high uncertainty, there are still ways to position portfolios to take advantage of growing divergences beneath the surface—between sectors, regions, and asset classes. Cutting through the noise means staying grounded in what is known, alert to what is changing, and prepared for what is next.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Auto Trouble Brewing Over Rare Earth Curbs
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Auto industry braces for supply chain disruptions from China's rare earth curbs. (00:21) Kimberly-Clark nears $3.5B sale of international tissue business to Suzano - WSJ. (01:35) Citigroup to cut 3,500 jobs at China tech centers. (02:33)

This is an abridged transcript.

The automotive industry is bracing for supply chain disruptions.

Suppliers are increasingly facing delays in getting export licenses from China due to controls on rare earth elements and magnets, which were imposed in retaliation to President Trump's tariffs.

China dominates the critical mineral supply chain, producing about 90% of the world's rare earths that are widely used in the auto, energy, semiconductor, and defense industries.

"The European automotive supply industry is already experiencing significant disruption," auto supplier association CLEPA has warned, adding that the impacted auto parts are critical to combustion engine and electric vehicles.

"These restrictions have led to the shutdown of several production lines and plants across Europe, with further impacts expected in the coming weeks as inventories deplete," the group noted.

While the European Union is in talks with China to address the issue, the bloc is looking to diversify supply chains. The EU has identified 13 critical raw material projects overseas to ensure sustainable supply, including in the UK, Ukraine, Greenland, Canada, and South Africa.

Kimberly-Clark (NASDAQ:KMB) is nearing a sale of its Kleenex and tissue businesses outside of North America.

The Wall Street Journal reported Wednesday that the buyer is Brazilian pulp producer Suzano (NYSE:SUZ) for ~$3.5B, in a deal that could be completed as soon as today.

The sale would be part of an attempt by Kimberly-Clark to focus on more profitable areas, according to the report.

Kimberly-Clark's (NASDAQ:KMB) global tissue business, known as the International Family Care & Professional segment, is the smallest of the company's three core divisions, with annual net sales totaling less than $3.5B and the lowest profit margins of the three.

A sale would leave Kimberly-Clark (KMB) with its North America business, the company's largest, with ~$11B in net sales last year, as well as its international personal care division, whose net sales amounted to ~$5.7B in 2024.

Citigroup (NYSE:C) will cut about 3,500 jobs at its two major technology centers in China.

The cuts at the China Citi Solution Centres in Shanghai and Dalian are expected to be completed by Q4 this year.

The jobs affected are mostly in the IT services unit, providing software technology development, testing and maintenance, and operational services for Citi’s global business. A few positions will be transferred to Citi's technology centers elsewhere.

This action is part of Citi's (NYSE:C) previously announced plan to reduce its global workforce by 20,000 by the end of 2026, as the bank seeks to streamline operations and cut costs amid a challenging global economic environment.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Trump signs travel ban for citizens from 12 countries, citing security concerns

Canada to hold off response on higher U.S. metals tariffs

Global energy investment to touch record $3.3 trillion in 2025, IEA says

Catalyst watch:

  • Management with Procter & Gamble (PG) and Levis Strauss (LEVI) will present at the Deutsche Bank dbAccess Global Consumer Conference.

  • Nintendo (OTCPK:NTDOY) will release the Switch 2 in the U.S. and Europe. The next-generation hybrid console builds on the versatility of the original Switch, while introducing significant upgrades in hardware, design, and features. Retailers Target (TGT), Best Buy (BBY), and Walmart (WMT) are expected to see an incremental sales lift.

  • Oshkosh (OSK) will hold an Investor Day event. Management will provide an in-depth review of the company's strategic initiatives, technology highlights, capital allocation priorities and financial growth targets.

  • Lyft (LYFT) will hold its annual meeting with Engine Capital pushing for its own board nominees.

Now let’s take a look at the markets ahead of the opening bell. Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 0.2% at $62/barrel. Bitcoin is up 0.1% at $104,000. Gold is up 0.6% at $3,393.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.2% and the DAX is up 0.3%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Verint Systems (NASDAQ:VRNT) +21% – Shares surged after the company delivered better-than-expected Q1 results, highlighted by a 24% Y/Y increase in AI annual recurring revenue (AI ARR), which includes its AI-powered software offerings.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Jobless Claims

  • 12:00 pm Federal Reserve Board Governor Adriana Kugler speaks on the economic outlook and monetary policy before an Economic Club of New York luncheon.

  • 1:30 pm Philadelphia Federal Reserve Bank President Patrick Harker speaks on the economic outlook before the Philadelphia Council for Business Economics.

  • 1:30 pm Kansas City Federal Reserve Bank President Jeffrey Schmid speaks on banking policy before"The Future of Banking: Navigating Change" conference hosted by the Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City.

data.title: Fast Retailing: Solid Earnings And Future Outlook - Buy
data.description:
I rate Fast Retailing (OTCPK:FRCOY) as a Buy, taking into consideration the upside potential following the stock's lackluster performance YTD (in line with the broader retail sector). Latest earnings were strong, and management's forward guidance was a net positive, in my opinion. I see more runway for growth in the European and US markets, where the company has yet to penetrate these markets meaningfully.

In this analysis, I will briefly touch on Fast Retailing's latest earnings release, full-year guidance revisions, and explain my thesis as to why Fast Retailing is a stock to look out for.

Latest Earnings Review

Fast Retailing's 2Q (Dec-Feb) consolidated results show sales of ¥895.0bn (+13.6% YoY) and operating profit at ¥146.6bn (+32.8%). For the quarter, UNIQLO Japan in particular saw strong sales +14.3% YoY and operating profit +48.1% (same-store sales +12.5%, GPM +2.1ppt). UNIQLO International sales were +15.8%, operating profit +16.3%.

To summarize, 2025 1H (Sep-Feb) revenue was ¥1,790.1bn (+12% YoY), business profit was ¥301.6bn (+20.1% YoY, ~¥20bn above company's initial guidance). The beat was attributable to strong performances for UNIQLO Japan and Europe. Segmental operating profit included UNIQLO Japan at ¥97.6bn (+26.4%), UNIQLO International at ¥168.5bn (+11.7%), GU at ¥13.9bn (-9.3%).

For 2H, management expects revenue and business profit to increase, but operating profit to dip slightly. Management's calculated impact of the tariffs, assuming all tax rates announced on 2 April are applied, and product prices will not be raised in 2H, will be roughly 2 to 3%.

Summary Table (For FY25)

Initial mgmt guided estimates for FY25 (Oct 24) Revised mgmt estimates (Apr 25) Change
Consol Revenue 3,400.0bn (+9.5% YoY) 3,400.0bn no change
Business profit 530.0bn (+5.8% YoY) 540.0bn +10bn
Net Profit 385.0bn (+3.5% YoY) 410.0bn +25bn
EPS 1,255.14 1,336.51 +111bps

Full year Forward Guidance:

  • Full-year targets were unchanged for sales, but management raised business profit from ¥530bn to ¥540bn (+11.3% YoY)
  • Slight upward revisions of ¥15bn for operating profit, ¥50bn for pre-tax profit.
  • Second-half business profit target has been cut by ¥10bn to allow for some tariff impact, but the FY target was raised in expectation of the 1H overshoot covering.

The main changes in the company's assumptions for 2H are:

  • Downward revisions for sales and OP in Greater China UNIQLO business after missing targets in 1H - management cited lack of appetite for apparel in general
  • Downward revision for the GU business after 1H sales growth disappointed - attributed to a lack of hit products that captured mass fashion trends and were unaffected by changes in the weather, as well as shortages
  • Downward revision for operating profit in the US UNIQLO business and Theory business - management has factored in the US tariff rates announced on Apr 2 are applied in full and merchandise prices are not raised; calculating to a 2-3% effect on 2H group business profit.
  • Management commented that they already have a substantial amount of stock in the USA, so the estimated impact will for 2H will be relatively muted.

Weakness in China More Than Offset by Japan and International (Ex-China) Sales Growth

The company has yet to penetrate Europe and the US markets meaningfully, and its stores are only present in certain cities. As per the latest sales data, UNIQLO Japan business continues strongly, with May SSS + online sales up +13.1% (customer traffic +12.5%; per-customer spend +0.5%). Directly operated stores + online sales were up +15.5%.

While I acknowledge the demand weakness in China, management highlighted and spoke about its additional challenges in China this quarter, citing challenges of developing product mixes to the differing climates and cultures across China's large land mass. Given such commentary, I believe the significant contributor to the worst-than-expected revenue decline this quarter was the company's inventory management and stocking issues, not on demand.

I see further room for growth internationally ex-China. Fast Retailing is actively expanding its store network to new regions, and is just getting started growing in Europe and the US. UNIQLO's market share is only <0.5% of the €250B+ (¥70 trillion) European apparel market. Despite strong growth (+44.5% YoY in Europe for FY24), UNIQLO remains a niche player compared to Zara (Inditex) and H&M; In the US, as of Feb 2025, H&M operates ~508 stores while UNIQLO operates just ~69 stores across the United States.

Management is also positive on in Europe/US markets, commenting that they are "witnessing a positive cycle in which new customers are flocking to UNIQLO stores as word of mouth recommendations, enhancing the brand's reputation." As such, we believe that double-digit profit growth is sustainable in the medium term, driven by the strength in Japan, growth in Europe and the US. Recent store openings include: the first UNIQLO Poland store opened in Warsaw, Poland in September, along with GU's U.S. debut in NYC SOHO.

Brand Collaborations Are a Core Strength for Fast Retailing

Fast Retailing's core strengths lies in UNIQLO's efforts in brand collaborations as part of its go-to market strategy. The unique blend of high-low partnerships drives brand heat while maintaining its "LifeWear" philosophy. UNIQLO has maintained its position as a collaboration powerhouse in the fast fashion market, leveraging partnerships with luxury designers, pop culture icons, and sports legends. While figures for individual collections are rarely disclosed, I believe that these collaborations blend cultural relevance, technical innovation, and strategic pricing to drive consistent sales growth.

I note some upcoming campaigns in 2025 that, in my opinion, will be well-received collaborations.

  • +J (Jil Sander Revival, 2024-2025)
  • UNIQLO POP MART UT Collection (April 2025)
  • The Roger Federer Collection (May 2025)
  • Pokémon UT (August 2025)

Beyond collaborations, UNIQLO has crafted its "LifeWear" philosophy on casual apparel, choosing to emphasize functional wear over fashion trends, at accessible price points. There is evidence of consumers trading down on luxury spending and scaling back on luxury goods; this bodes well for UNIQLO. As per Bain & Company's forecasts, luxury goods sales are set to show a 2-5% contraction in sales this year, a stark reversal from earlier expectations of modest growth.

I note that the intensity and timing of seasonal changes are always discussed in the earnings commentaries - in the sense that volume/sell-through is correlated to seasonal weather, thereby making UNIQLO's seasonal offerings more of a necessity rather than a fashion statement. Seasonal innovations like UV-resistant fabrics and Airism cooling technologies cater to warm weathers, while HEATTECH and its thermal clothing line to cold weathers.

Management's Estimated 2–3% Downward Effect on US UNIQLO Operating Profit for FY25 Sounds Prudent

Management has commented that a reasonable amount of merchandise has already been stocked domestically in the USA in 2H. By my estimates, in a worst-case scenario, I calculate that the potential cost increases from reciprocal tariffs to contribute a -3.9% downward effect on operating profit.

The company's key manufacturing hubs are concentrated in China, Vietnam, Bangladesh, Indonesia, and India, with additional facilities in other Southeast Asian countries. I dive deeper into the company's publicly available Garment Factory and Processing Factory List as of March 2025, and find that of the 380 garment factories on the list, 206 are located in China, followed by 60 facilities in Vietnam, and Bangladesh coming in third with 27 facilities.

To compute the above figure, I make the assumption that product prices will not be raised in 2H (reiterated by management) and assume that there will be no cost pass through in the supply chain. I also make the assumption that the company will have zero exports from China to the US, and that Vietnam (46% additional tariff) will be the sole producer for the US market.

Geographic breakdown shows that North America business accounts for ~7.7% of total revenues. I grow this figure with the forecasted growth rate of consolidated revenue, 9.5%, to derive a forecasted 8.4% of North America sales contribution. I multiply this figure with 46% (the additional tariff on Vietnam) to derive a figure of c.3.9% cost impact, assuming no other COGS variable changes.

Among other considerations, our calculations do not account for secondary effects, such as the risk of economic slowdown in countries subject to high reciprocal tariffs due to lower production for the US market, and slower US consumption due to tariff-driven inflation.

Tariff Announcement Caused Only a Mild Negative Reaction in Fast Retailing's Stock Price

Following the US government's April 2nd announcement (local time) of tariffs on imports from a range of countries, On April 3, share prices fell 9.9% day-on-day for ASICS and 1.7% for Fast Retailing, while the TOPIX was down by 3.1%. I believe this reflects investor's confidence in Fast Retailing's business model, competitive strengths and management.

Valuation

As of the time of writing, Fast Retailing YTD return is down approximately ~-9.4% versus the TOPIX Index of ~+1.6%. Fast Retailing's 1212-monthorward P/E premium versus other global brands that generate large earnings in China is above the average premium since 2019, but I see this as appropriate given the rapid rise in market share in US/Europe and Southeast Asia. I believe that the company's growth potential is not fully factored into the current share price, given its negative return YTD.

The stock currently trades at a 12m forward P/E of 36.1x, below its forward P/E multiple of 40.8x at the start of the year. Considering the forecast hike in operating profit and the potential for tariff reprieve, I believe that the company should trade around its prior P/E multiples and derive our 12m price target of ¥55,000 (US-Listed ADR $38.12), based on a management guided EPS of 1,336.51, target P/E of 41x.

Key Risks

I believe that the key risks revolve around tariff uncertainties and its ripple effects on the global economy. Management's current stance is to not raise prices and absorb the tariff cost, which will impact margins for the US business considering that most of the manufacturing works are done in Asia. The negative impact of any trade war escalation will lower consumer confidence, impacting consumer spending across geographies. Intensifying competition with disruptors like Shein, legacy rivals like Zara and H&M. Internal factors that may affect the company's performance include: delays in launching new products, insufficient product launches and poor inventory control.

Conclusion

Fast Retailing's "LifeWear" philosophy differentiates it from pure fast-fashion competitors through technology-driven basics and premium positioning. While Inditex excels in trend-driven fashion cycles and H&M competes on price, Fast Retailing occupies the quality-value intersection with superior operational margins. The company's international expansion strategy, particularly in Europe and North America, provides significant growth runway.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: National Australia Bank: The Good And The Bad
data.description:
I remain Neutral on National Australia Bank (OTCPK:NABZY) (OTCPK:NAUBF) (NAB.AX). NABZY's housing loans segment and cash distributions were in the spotlight for my prior July 9, 2024, update.

NABZY still warrants a Hold. The stock is at a reasonable valuation, and financial prospects are mixed.

Mortgage Outperformance and Deposit Share Gains Are Positives

Household deposits (HHD) for NABZY expanded at an annualized rate of 10.6% in the six months ended April 30, 2025. This figure is taken from the latest APRA (Australian Prudential Regulation Authority) data released at the end of May.

For the Nov 24 to Apr 25 period, NABZY's HHD growth was the fastest among major AU (Australian) banks and 180 bps (basis points) better than its closest rival Westpac (OTCPK:WEBNF).

NABZY also revealed at the 1HFY25 (YE September 30, 2025) call (source: S&P Capital IQ transcript) last month that it boasts the "highest market share of all peers" in "business deposits ('BD')" at 22 percent now. The bank was previously ranked 3rd in this category, so that's a big improvement.

New Payment-Related Offerings For Corporate Clients

NABZY saw its BD increase with the roll-out of fresh innovations that make enterprise money transfers easier, as outlined above.

Also, the firm has strengthened its customer-facing operations to garner more HHD. This is evidenced by a near one-third YoY rise in individual account openings at the bank's physical branches for 1HFY2025.

NABZY had described BD/HHD as an area of "historical weakness" in its corporate presentation. The company's funding costs should trend downwards over time, as it grabs a bigger slice of the deposit pie.

Separately, NABZY's mortgages grew by 6.3% on an annualized basis in April this year, as per APRA's statistics I referred to above. The same figure for WEBNF was an inferior +1.6%.

Its YoY home loan volume expansion had already improved from 0.7% in 2HFY24 to 2.3% for 1HFY25 (October 1, 2024, to March 31, 2025).

In the past, NABZY was overly dependent on third-party intermediaries (brokers) to hunt for fresh mortgage opportunities. This has changed substantially, with its "proprietary (self-sourced) drawdowns" metric up 14% HoH (Half-on-Half) and 25% YoY in the recent interim period. In its investor presentation, the bank disclosed that it recently set up a "dedicated team for proprietary home lending."

It's likely that NABZY's positive mortgage momentum will continue, as the sourcing mix optimization bears fruit.

The good things to note about NABZY is its home lending growth and deposit share increases.

But Profitability Could Take a Hit From Higher Expenses and Stiffer Competition

The downside risks associated with margins are the major negatives for NABZY.

NABZY's normalized (adjusted for Markets and Treasury) NIM (Net Interest Margin) contracted by three bps sequentially in the first half of fiscal 2025.

The firm indicated at its May-25 analyst briefing that "competitive pressures" in "business lending" contributed a third of the decline, or -0.01 ppt (percentage points). It also acknowledged the increase in "activity from our competitors in the SME (Small and Medium-sized Enterprises) space" in recent months.

AU is witnessing a declining rate environment, with a 25 bps cut happening in the previous month. It's natural that domestic banks are pivoting towards higher-margin small business lending to boost their NIMs. NABZY stands to be the biggest loser. Other financial firms see it as the main rival due to its leading 25% (source: earnings call transcript) SME share.

Also, rising costs might hurt its profitability.

NABZY's OPEX (Operating Expenses) went up by 5% in the most recent full fiscal year (FY24). The bank's CIR (Operating Costs-to-Income) ratio also increased from 34 percent for 2HFY2024 to 40% in 1HFY25.

NABZY is unlikely to trim its spending in certain areas that are needed to maintain competitive parity with its rivals. At its 1HFY2025 results call, the bank drew attention to "the ongoing investment in modernizing our technology".

AU businesses in general, including NABZY, will have to contend with rising labor expenses going forward. Australian newspaper AFR's June 3, 2025, news article mentioned that the nation's "minimum wage" was raised by "3.5%" in view of "soaring inflation."

The sell-side (source: S&P Capital IQ consensus) sees NIM and net profit margin for NABZY declining by -1.1 ppts and 6 bps, respectively in the next two years (FY25-26). I think this is realistic, taking into account the challenges discussed here.

My Conclusion

I retain my existing Hold rating for National Australia Bank.

NABZY's valuations are on par with its Australian comparable. NABZY offers a 4.4% forward dividend yield and trades at 16.9x (Next Twelve Months) P/E as per S&P Capital IQ data.

Westpac's earnings multiple and dividend-to-price ratio are similar at 16.6 times and 4.6 percent, respectively.

The outlook for NABZY is murky according to my assessment of the group's mortgage operations, deposits, NIMs, and expenses. There is an absence of well-defined catalysts to justify a re-rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: European Banks' Loan Loss Provisions Jump 18% YOY Amid Tariff Risks
data.description:
Europe's biggest banks reported an 18% year-over-year increase in provisions for loan losses during the first quarter, amid warnings that uncertainty around international trade policy could hurt asset quality.

Aggregate provisions among 45 of the region's largest lenders rose to €11.48 billion from €9.72 billion in the first quarter of 2024 and €8.45 billion a year earlier.

On a quarterly basis, provisions fell 5.8% to €11.48 billion from €12.19 billion in the fourth quarter of 2024.

While euro area banks continue to display robust asset quality, heightened trade tensions may cause nonperforming loans (NPLs) and provisioning costs to increase, the European Central Bank said in its May 13 Financial Stability Review. Banks with higher exposure to sectors relying on extra-EU trade could be particularly affected, the regulator noted.

Market uncertainty has also raised concerns about the way the region's lenders provision for emerging risks. Extraordinary events, such as the hefty US tariff hike in early April and the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, have exposed the limitations of standard models used to estimate potential credit losses, resulting in large differences on the banks' interpretation of risk and their provisioning.

Seventeen of the 45 banks reported a quarterly increase in their problem loan ratios, and 18 reported a year-over-year increase.

Four of the five UK-headquartered banks in the sample posted higher problem loan ratios, with Barclays PLC (BCS) registering the sharpest increase. All are projected to hike provisions this year, Visible Alpha data shows.

Barclays set a £74 million provision for risks linked to US macroeconomic uncertainty, although it noted that its portfolios had not yet shown signs of deterioration, with delinquencies broadly stable in the UK and in the US.

Lloyds Banking Group plc's (LYG) first quarter provisions jumped to £309 million from £57 million a year prior, of which £100 million was attributed to tariff risks.

"To be very clear, this is not addressing any impacts that we're seeing today within our book... This is about getting ahead of what might develop and making sure that we are suitably provisioned," Lloyds CFO William Chalmers said on the bank's earnings call.

France's Groupe BPCE and Italy's UniCredit SpA (OTCPK:UNCRY) recorded the largest quarterly increases in their problem loan ratios. UniCredit will need to set aside about €800 million in further loan loss provisions to align with the asset quality of its takeover target, Banco BPM SpA (OTCPK:BNCZF), CEO Andrea Orcel said during the bank's May 12 earnings call.

Mediobanca Banca di Credito Finanziario SpA (OTCPK:MDIBY) achieved the biggest quarterly reduction at 48 bps. It is the subject of a takeover bid from Italian peer Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena SpA (OTCPK:BMDPF), which had the highest problem loan ratio in the sample at 4.01%.

Barclays, Sweden's Skandinaviska Enskilda Banken AB (publ) (OTCPK:SVKEF) and UniCredit recorded the biggest quarterly rises in Stage 2 loan ratios. Stage 2 loans are those with a significant increase in credit risk since initial recognition.

SEB booked 663 million Swedish kronor in loan loss provisions in the first quarter, up from 73 million a year ago, mainly due to select corporate clients in certain sectors, President and CEO Johan Torgeby said on the bank's earnings call. Stage 2 exposures rose at SEB following an update to its retail expected credit loss models, according to the bank.

While Sweden is more exposed to tariffs than other Nordic countries, SEB and its two smaller peers in the sample, Swedbank AB (publ) (OTCPK:SWDBF) and Svenska Handelsbanken AB (publ) (OTCPK:SVNLF), recorded stable bad loan ratios in the first quarter.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MNTN's IPO May Be Over - But The Opportunity Isn't
data.description:
With this analysis, I am beginning my coverage on MNTN, Inc. (NYSE:MNTN), a newly listed adtech company. In this analysis, I'll be providing an overview of the company, their business model, and recent financial performance. I'll also be offering color regarding their current valuation and highlighting the primary risks that I see for the company. In the end, I'll be recommending a 'Buy' rating for the stock for long-term investors, for reasons discussed throughout.

Company Overview

Advertising on television has always seemed like big business. And historically, it has been. Only the companies with the deepest pockets had the means to reach nationwide audiences via television commercials. The high costs and lack of precision targeting have created a rather narrow set of qualified advertisers.

Every February, headlines hit the news outlets calling out the absurd prices for 30-second ad spots during the Super Bowl. This past year, such a spot cost upwards of $7 million, or close to $600,000 per second of air time. The largest brands have ponied up for 30-second ad spots that cost more than the lifetime earnings of the average American citizen—several times over.

Headlines such as these have woven the narrative that advertising on television is accessible for only the largest of brands. Small and medium-sized businesses have been relegated to the position that they need to work themselves up the advertising ladder before they can take advantage of the unparalleled scale offered by television advertising. They allocate their advertising spend to social media, display advertising, email marketing, radio, print, billboards, etc. Then, only once they have reached critical mass, they can make the jump to advertising on television.

Traditionally, this has been the norm for the advertising industry. However, there are a select few companies that have begun shaking things up the status quo. One of those companies is MNTN.

MNTN was founded in 2009, coming out of the depths of the global financial crisis. The company is headquartered in Austin, Texas. MNTN's software enables businesses of all sizes to run advertising on television with the same ease that you'd expect from other forms of paid advertising, such as social media. Moreover, MNTN's software allows these businesses to run targeted, performance-driven campaigns—a task that has historically proved elusive in the big black box world of television advertising.

The company went public via an IPO just a few weeks ago. Shares had an offering price of $16, but the stock opened at $21. Shares continued to pop, as they jumped as much as 64% on the opening day.

Over the next few days, shares peaked close to $30. The market has since cooled down a bit on MNTN, with shares trading for ~$25. The company was looking to raise $187.2 from the offering.

Financials

Over the last four quarters, MNTN generated $246.3 million of revenue. This total is up 9.2% year-over-year. For context, in fiscal year 2021, MNTN posted just under $100 million of total revenue.

It's also worth noting that MNTN generates the core of their revenue from a take rate on total media spend on their platform. They also offer customers access to their in-house creative agency to help create ads, but this wouldn't be considered as their core revenue stream. While the take rate monetization model has its pros, it will be important to gauge the durability of MNTN's revenue should any macroeconomic headwinds arise.

From their revenue, MNTN posted a gross profit of 72%, indicating healthy unit economics. Moreover, MNTN produced a modest operating profit over the last year of $5 million. They are right in the middle of their transition to profitability.

Although they are yet to reach profitability, they are indeed cash flow positive. MNTN generated $52.6 million of cash from operations over the last year. In fiscal year 2024, they posted $42.5 million of cash from operations. In fiscal year 2023, this number was $18 million. Growth in cash generation from operations has been strong, largely due to operating leverage within the company.

As of their latest quarterly report, MNTN had $82.3 million of total cash and short-term investments booked on their balance sheet. However, I'd imagine this number is set to go up significantly following their IPO. They raised close to $200 million from the offering, so I'd expect their cash balance to be around $250 million come Q2 earnings. For an asset-light company generating around $250 million of annual revenue, this level of cash on their balance sheet will offer MNTN good optionality and serve as an initial cushion for riding the inherently volatile wave of the public markets.

Overall, MNTN's financials appear strong. Revenue growth has been strong leading up to their IPO. Continuing this trend will be key to sustained success in the public markets, too.

Valuation

With MNTN yet to reach profitability, revenue-based metrics provide the most context regarding their valuation. At the current price, MNTN is trading for an EV/Revenue multiple of 8.4x. I have to admit, this sounds a bit high considering their sub-10% revenue growth over the last year. However, it's worth pointing out that year-over-year revenue growth in fiscal year 2024 was much higher, at 28%.

An interesting company to compare with MNTN is The Trade Desk, Inc. (TTD). Much like MNTN, TTD is a software-based ad buying platform that enables businesses to purchase advertising across various verticals including display, video, audio, television, etc. TTD is a much larger business in terms of magnitude ($2.57 billion of TTM revenue), but it can still offer color regarding MNTN's valuation.

Currently, TTD is trading at an EV/Revenue multiple of 13.7x. This is over 5 turns higher than MNTN's valuation. Moreover, fiscal year 2024 growth at TTD (25.6%) is similar to MNTN (28%).

Although I do believe that TTD's greater size and maturity should merit it a premium valuation relative to MNTN, I do believe that the delta between the valuations of the companies indicates that MNTN's valuation has room to run—assuming they continue to perform well.

To add to all this, I believe that MNTN could be an opportunistic acquisition target for a handful of companies. Possible acquirers that come to mind are TTD, AppLovin (APP), and the large tech advertisers including Alphabet (GOOG), Meta (META), and Amazon (AMZN). While an acquisition of MNTN's size would be difficult to move the needle for the largest three companies (Alphabet, Meta, Amazon), it could appear attractive for TTD and AppLovin.

In all, I believe that MNTN is trading at a discount. They have put together strong growth and appear to be well-received by the public markets. In a time when the IPO waters are far from smooth sailing, shares of MNTN have been a vote of confidence for the company.

Risks

As I see it, the primary risk for MNTN has to do with their line of business and their set of customers. MNTN operates in the advertising industry, and it's no secret that the industry can be inherently volatile. In times of economic distress, advertising spend is often one of the first line items scrutinized by companies. As such, MNTN's business is inherently cyclical.

Moreover, MNTN caters primarily to SMBs and mid-market companies. This segment of customers comes with its own perks, but they're also the ones that are often most susceptible to macro headwinds. When the seas get rough, it's often the smallest ships that have the hardest time navigating the rough waters.

Investors should monitor potential macro headwinds to get a better feel for the trajectory of MNTN's business. Additionally, investors should pay close attention to management's sentiment regarding these factors in their first earnings call as a public company in Q2.

Conclusion

In an industry that has long been reserved for the largest of corporations, MNTN has set out to democratize access to television advertising to even the smallest of businesses. They've been at this mission for over a decade and a half and have built a strong business. Top-line growth has been strong, and the company is on the cusp of profitability. In addition, MNTN's valuation appears reasonable relative to peers in the industry. As such, I am rating the stock a 'Buy' for long-term investors.

data.title: Koppers Meeting Its Aggressive Guidance So Far, But I Wait For A Lower Price
data.description:
Koppers' (KOP) 1Q25 results from last month were surprisingly in line with the very aggressive guidance issued at the end of 2024.

The company showed margin recovery in RUPS and CM&C, which compensated for a steep drop in PC segment earnings. This was all previously guided, and while results are volatile, they are not surprising. The quarter registered an accrual net loss, but this was largely driven by non-cash impairments and pension settlements.

With a more solid path to guidance achievement, Koppers may look undervalued on a forward P/E ratio. Still, I believe the long-term normalized earnings are lower than current projections. I maintain my view that the stock becomes attractive below $25 and remains a Hold for now.

1Q25 results

As commented across my coverage of KOP (see, for example, February 2024), the company's three segments average out margin cycles that are classical in industrial and chemical markets.
