For FY25, management had guided in 4Q24 that Performance Chemicals (its most profitable and stable segment) would see a large decline in revenue and earnings due to increased competition, and that this would be offset by cyclical recovery in RUPS and CM&C. Back in 4Q24, I considered this an aggressive assumption. But so far in Q1, that playbook has held.

Starting with PC, the segment posted a sharp revenue decline and significant margin compression. This came primarily from the entry of new domestic competitors undercutting on price. Koppers is choosing not to match lower prices, and is losing share. This reflects typical cyclical dynamics (overcapacity leads to lower margins), but is especially concerning here because PC has long been Koppers' most profitable and consistent business. The hit to earnings is likely to last a few years. Additionally, economic uncertainty in the U.S. following "Liberation Day" tariffs led to a pause in residential construction and reform activity, adding cyclical pressure to a structurally weaker market.

Railroads and Utility Poles (RUPS), on the other hand, is delivering as promised. The company raised prices for railroad crossties and expanded utility pole volume, aided by the Brown Wood acquisition last year. These products are tied to long-term infrastructure needs, and demand tends to average out over time. After a weak 2023-24, demand is gradually recovering, and margins are improving from higher utilization and contract renegotiations. The mix shift toward utility poles, which have higher margins than rail, is also positive, as commented during the 1Q25 call.

The most positive surprise came from Chemicals and Carbon (CM&C). Despite continued top-line declines and global oversupply, EBITDA margins rose from 3% to 9%. This was driven by significant cost-cutting and the full exit from the phthalic anhydride business. While I continue to view CM&C as the structurally weakest segment, the improvement is notable and aligns with the company's strategy of simplifying operations.

Non-recurring factors: Two factors impacted the accrual EPS this quarter. One was the settlement of a pension plan, impacting $30 million in pension plan assets, at no cash cost according to the 10-Q. The second was a $20 million impairment charge, of which $13 million is non-cash (D&A acceleration) while the rest is cash-based (severance, consulting).

Questionable capital allocation priorities: Koppers' net debt increased by $60 million this quarter. Management attributed this to seasonal working capital build in Q1. That's consistent with industrial patterns, but it's worth highlighting that the company also repurchased $20 million in stock and paid $1.4 million in dividends. I do not support the decision to buy back stock while leverage remains elevated (3.6x EBITDA).

That said, CapEx is being tightly managed. The company expects only $65-70 million in net capital outlays for FY25, almost entirely for maintenance.

Still, I would prefer the company to use most available cash after maintenance to repay debt, and only when the leverage risk is reduced, to return cash to shareholders via dividends or buybacks. Leverage reduction is particularly important as the economy becomes more uncertain.

Valuation Is Reasonable, But I Remain Patient

Koppers is guiding to $4.75 in adjusted EPS for FY25. This includes adjustments for non-recurring items like the ones mentioned above, but not too much more. It is therefore approximately reflective of the company's expected operations this year. If this guidance is met, then at $32 per share, the stock is cheap on a 6/7x multiple.

However, I continue to believe that current margins are above the long-term average, particularly given that the most profitable segment is entering a down cycle. Based on previous periods (see detailed explanation in 3Q24 article), I estimate normalized earnings per share closer to $3.5. This is based on an average return on assets, adjusted for the company's higher asset base. However, this calculation is somewhat optimistic because it is based on periods when the PC segment was less oversupplied.

If margins revert further in FY25 or FY26, the current debt load will also become a bigger concern. Leverage is already elevated, and a drop in EBITDA could make refinancing or capital allocation decisions harder. I would prefer to see proactive deleveraging while cash flows remain relatively healthy.

Overall, I like where Koppers is going strategically and believe the market is starting to reflect a more realistic valuation. But I continue to believe that cyclical weakness will pressure the name further. It is very normal in cyclical industrial or chemical names for prices to really undershoot on the way down. For that reason, I maintain my Hold rating and prefer to wait for a drop below $25 before considering a position.
